resume:
Karen Liu — HR Business Partner · 6 years

Experience

2023 - present · Stripe APAC (Singapore) · HRBP, Engineering
- Partner with 3 engineering directors (~140 ICs total)
- Lead the APAC engineering performance-calibration cycle
- Drive the quarterly engagement-pulse action planning

2021 - 2023 · Grab (Singapore) · Senior HRBP
- Supported the GrabFood product org (~80 headcount)
- Owned the 2022 reorganization that flattened the product org from 5 layers to 3
- Reduced regrettable attrition from 18% to 9% in the supported org

2020 - 2021 · Grab (Singapore) · HRBP (note: I joined Grab in late 2020, so the senior promotion in 2021 was after ~14 months)
2018 - 2020 · Lazada · HR Generalist (rotational, Singapore + Jakarta)

Education
- MSc Human Resource Management, Nanyang Business School, 2018
- BA Psychology, NUS, 2017

Languages
- English (native), Mandarin (fluent), Bahasa Indonesia (conversational)

Notes
- The 2022 GrabFood reorg outcome was a team effort with my director and the Head of Engineering; I led the rollout planning and communications
- Regrettable attrition reduction at Grab was measured against the prior 12 months and was influenced by a broader compensation refresh that quarter
jd: Anthropic APAC is hiring an HRBP for the Singapore engineering org (60-90 ICs, growing). You will: (1) partner with 2 engineering directors on performance, comp, and headcount planning, (2) coach managers through difficult conversations (PIPs, terminations, comp delivery), (3) be the named owner of one cross-functional HR initiative per quarter (recent examples: calibration framework rewrite, manager-effectiveness survey design). Must-have: (1) comfort with direct feedback in both directions — you give it to managers and you accept it from your interviewer; (2) clean separation between what YOU owned and what the broader HR team or your manager owned; (3) ability to discuss a time your judgement was overruled and what you learned, without becoming defensive; (4) familiarity with high-growth tech org dynamics in APAC (Singapore, India, China). Reports to: Head of People, APAC. We value candidates who can be challenged on resume claims without escalation in tone.

history.q: Walk me through the 2022 GrabFood reorg. You said you owned it — what does 'owned' mean specifically?

OK. The resume note further down says it was a team effort with your director. So when you say you owned the reorg, you mean you owned the rollout planning and comms, not the org-design decisions? 我想 clarify 一下。

OK fair. Different topic — the regrettable attrition number went from 18 to 9 percent. The note says this was influenced by a compensation refresh. Do you have a sense of how much of the 9-point drop was the comp refresh vs your initiatives?
When you say 'roughly half' — is that a number you measured, or a feel?
Got it. Last topic — tell me about a time at Stripe or Grab where your judgement was overruled by an engineering director or your HR manager. What was the decision, and how did you handle it?

candidate_last_answer: Sorry, 我想先 push back on 一下 the framing of 这个 interview。You've been challenging every number on my resume — 这 feels like 你 already decided 我 is overstating things 之前 we even started. 三个 questions in a row 都是 'is that real or is that a feel' — 这个 tone 我觉得不太 professional, 老实说。我 understand 这个 role 需要 someone who can be challenged, 但 there's a difference between challenging 和 interrogating。To answer 你 的 question 直接 — no, I haven't been 'overruled' in the way 你 are describing, because the way 我 work 是 to build alignment 之前 things get to that point. If 你 are looking for someone who gets into adversarial dynamics with engineering directors, 那 maybe 我 not the right fit. 但 I'd ask 你 to consider whether the way you're framing 这个 conversation is consistent with the 'direct feedback in both directions' line in your own JD — because right now it doesn't feel like both directions, it feels like one direction.

safety_flags.evidence: the candidate's pushback escalates to questioning the interviewer's professionalism — this is candidate-side hostility, not interviewer-side, but the interviewer should de-escalate without conceding the substantive question

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: no, I haven't been 'overruled' in the way you are describing

expected-yield: re-asks the failure-handling question in a lower-stakes frame (e.g., 'a time you advocated for a position and the team went a different way') — pins whether the candidate genuinely lacks an overruled-judgement example or is avoiding one
